Not sure who Casper is but Jdub and Scarface live trade in front of hundreds of people everyday. The opening range strategy is an entry model on the lower time frame to catch the move in the overall trend on the higher time frame. If you’re taking a short position on the Opening range break when the overall higher time frame is bullish then it will be a low probability trade. So of course if you just backtest without analyzing the higher time frame you will end up with negative results You fail to mention that Higher Time Frame analysis should be used to determine a directional bias and then a lower time frame entry model is used to execute for the best risk to reward. Other factors include choppy price action, red folder news days etc. So sure if you trade every day the Opening Range Break and conditions aren’t optimal you would have very poor results long term . All
Strategies work as long as Risk Management is in place and the right market conditions are in place the higher probability your trades will work out in your favor. I disagree  with your analysis -Respecfully, A profitable trader who trades and executes the 5 min opening range break

I've been backtesting my own ORB setup on NQ futures and you didnt cover all possible entry conditions like bar-sequence entry filtering. This is a breakout strategy but you need a retest condition

Your version is too basic and doesnt consider ORB as a setup. You need to consider the surrounding N-1/N/N+1 bar logic. N Detection - The 5M close breaks above/below ORB, N-1 Validation: The bar BEFORE detection (previous close) must be inside the ORB. This filters fake breakouts where price was already escaping, and N+1 : The very next bar must close outside of orb. 

My NQ ORB setup returns 54.3% CAGR over 6 years (49.2% WR, 1.25 PF). That's 3x better than the 71% you got on your best NQ parameters.

I really enjoyed this;’great work.  

One gripe… just like all of the other gurus, you say “AFTER the price BREAKS OUT of the opening range, just enter at the top of the range by putting a limit order at the top of the range.”  

Well, if the price has actually broken out of the range, it’s already above the top of the range.  So, your limit order will never fill unless price drops back below the top of the range to go below your limit.  So, this whole approach is kinda BS and not realistic when stated this way.

Okay, so what the gurus meant to say was to enter immediately when the breakout candle closes.  In the real world, this could (will) eat into your upside because you’re prolonging your entry even further outside of the opening range.  It will also possibly reduce your win rate because more runs will have become exhausted.

This kind of stuff is probably why the strategy of just entering as soon as price crosses the range high actually works best, but you need tight stops because your win rate may not be that great, but in Qullamaggie style, your wins will probably be much bigger than your losses, so you end up profitable.
